stem: Let \( V = \operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \mathbf{v}_3, \mathbf{v}_4\} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^3 \), where \[ \mathbf{v}_1 = (1,\ [[s]],\ [[t]]),\quad \mathbf{v}_2 = ([[p]],\ [[m22]],\ [[m23]]),\quad \mathbf{v}_3 = ([[q]],\ [[m32]],\ [[m33]]),\quad \mathbf{v}_4 = ([[v4a]],\ [[v4b]],\ [[v4c]]). \] What is \( \dim V \)?
solution: The dimension of the span is the rank of the matrix whose rows are \( \mathbf{v}_1,\dots,\mathbf{v}_4 \). Row-reducing shows \( [[answer]] \) independent vectors, so \( \dim V = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This counts the four given vectors; the span's dimension cannot exceed \( 3 \) here.
This undercounts the independent vectors by one.
This overcounts the independent vectors by one.
This assumes the vectors fill all of \( \mathbb{R}^3 \) without checking dependence.
This treats the vectors as if they were all zero.
This undercounts the independent vectors.
This miscounts the number of independent vectors.
This is the number of redundant vectors, not the dimension of the span.

stem: Five vectors span a subspace of \( \mathbb{R}^3 \): \[ \mathbf{v}_1 = (1,\ [[s]],\ [[t]]),\ \mathbf{v}_2 = ([[p]],\ [[m22]],\ [[m23]]),\ \mathbf{v}_3 = ([[q]],\ [[m32]],\ [[m33]]),\ \mathbf{v}_4 = ([[v4a]],\ [[v4b]],\ [[v4c]]),\ \mathbf{v}_5 = (2,\ [[v5b]],\ [[v5c]]). \] What is the smallest number of these five vectors that must be discarded so that the vectors which remain still span the same subspace and form a basis of it?
solution: Note \( \mathbf{v}_5 = 2\mathbf{v}_1 \), so it is automatically redundant. Among \( \mathbf{v}_1,\dots,\mathbf{v}_4 \), row reduction shows only \( [[rankval]] \) are independent, so a basis for the span uses \( [[rankval]] \) of the five vectors and \( 5 - [[rankval]] = [[answer]] \) must be discarded.
distractor_rationales: This is the DIMENSION of the span (the number of vectors to KEEP), not the number to discard.
This assumes all five vectors are independent.
This assumes the vectors already form a basis with none redundant.
This overcounts the number to discard by one.
This undercounts the number to discard by one.
This forgets to count \( \mathbf{v}_5 \) as one of the vectors that could be discarded.
This overcounts the number to discard by two.

stem: Let \( \{\mathbf{v}_1, \dots, \mathbf{v}_{[[k]]}\} \) be a linearly independent set of vectors in \( \mathbb{R}^{[[n]]} \). What is the minimum number of additional vectors needed to extend this set to a basis of \( \mathbb{R}^{[[n]]} \)?
solution: Every basis of \( \mathbb{R}^{[[n]]} \) has exactly \( [[n]] \) vectors, and the Steinitz exchange (basis extension) theorem guarantees the independent set can be extended by adding vectors one at a time. Starting from \( [[k]] \) independent vectors, exactly \( [[n]] - [[k]] = [[answer]] \) more are needed.
distractor_rationales: This repeats the number already given, as if none more were needed beyond doubling them.
This is the full dimension of the space, as if none of the given vectors counted.
This adds instead of subtracting the given count from the dimension.
This overcounts the vectors still needed by one.
This undercounts the vectors still needed by one.
This overcounts the vectors still needed by two.
This is off by one from the given independent count, not the number still needed.

stem: Consider the polynomials \( p_1(x) = 1 + [[s]]x + [[t]]x^2 \), \( p_2(x) = [[p]] + [[m22]]x + [[m23]]x^2 \), and \( p_3(x) = [[q]] + [[m32]]x + [[m33]]x^2 \), all lying in \( P_2 \), the space of polynomials of degree at most \( 2 \). What is \( \dim(\operatorname{span}\{p_1, p_2, p_3\}) \)?
solution: Identify each polynomial with its coefficient vector \( (a_0, a_1, a_2) \) relative to the basis \( \{1, x, x^2\} \) of \( P_2 \); the dimension of the span equals the rank of the matrix of these coefficient vectors, which is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This exceeds the ambient dimension of \( P_2 \), which is only \( 3 \).
This undercounts the independent polynomials by one.
This overcounts the independent polynomials by one.
This counts all three polynomials as independent without checking.
This treats all three polynomials as identically zero.
This undercounts the independent polynomials by two.
This assumes only one polynomial is independent regardless of the coefficients.
This is the number of redundant polynomials, not the dimension of their span.
